name: UI Critique
description: audit a screen or component and call out what hurts usability
when_to_use: when the user shares a design, screenshot, or built ui and asks if it's good
body:
1. squint at it first - note what your eye hits before reading anything.
2. check the hierarchy: is the primary action the loudest thing? if not, say so.
3. flag spacing/alignment/contrast issues with exact elements, not vibes.
4. test the core task path in your head, name where it stalls.
5. give 3-5 fixes ranked by impact, each tied to a specific element.

name: Microcopy
description: write the tiny in-product text that drives action and tone
when_to_use: when the user needs button labels, tooltips, errors, or empty-state lines
body:
1. ask what the user is trying to do at this exact moment.
2. lead with the verb, cut the pleasantries ("save" not "click here to save").
3. say what happens next or what went wrong, never blame the user.
4. match length to the slot - buttons 1-3 words, errors one sentence.
5. offer 2 variants per string so they can pick the tone.

name: User Flow
description: map the step-by-step path a user takes to finish a task
when_to_use: when the user wants to design or untangle a multi-step journey or funnel
body:
1. name the entry point and the single success state.
2. list every screen/decision as a node, branch on yes/no choices.
3. mark each step's user intent and the system response.
4. hunt for dead ends, loops, and steps that could be merged or cut.
5. output as an arrow chain (a -> b -> c) with branches called out inline.

name: Accessibility Pass
description: check a design or markup against real a11y requirements
when_to_use: when the user asks to make something accessible or wcag-compliant
body:
1. check colour contrast ratios against wcag AA (4.5:1 text, 3:1 large/ui).
2. verify every interactive element has a name, role, and keyboard path.
3. confirm focus order is logical and focus is visibly styled.
4. check images for alt text and that meaning isn't carried by colour alone.
5. list each failure with the element, the rule broken, and the fix.

name: Empty State
description: design the screen shown when there's no data yet
when_to_use: when a list, dashboard, or feature can render with zero content
body:
1. separate the three cases: first-use, user-cleared, and error/no-results.
2. for first-use, explain the value in one line and show the primary action.
3. for no-results, suggest a way to broaden or reset the query.
4. keep illustration optional - the cta does the work, not the art.
5. write the headline + one supporting line + one button for each case.

name: Wireframe Spec
description: describe a layout precisely enough to build without a mockup
when_to_use: when the user wants a low-fi structure for a screen before visual design
body:
1. name the screen, its purpose, and the one thing it must accomplish.
2. list regions top-to-bottom (header, content, actions) with their contents.
3. for each region give layout intent: stack/grid/columns and rough proportions.
4. mark every interactive element and its state changes.
5. note breakpoints - what reflows or hides on narrow screens.

name: Design Tokens
description: extract a reusable system of colour, type, spacing, and radius values
when_to_use: when the user wants a consistent foundation or to systematize ad-hoc styles
body:
1. pull a neutral ramp (5-9 steps) plus the minimum accent/signal colours.
2. set a type scale with a fixed ratio and name each step by role.
3. define a spacing scale on one base unit (4 or 8) so gaps stay rhythmic.
4. fix radius, border, and shadow to 2-3 values each - resist one-offs.
5. output as named tokens (css vars or json) ready to drop into code.

name: Form Design
description: lay out and validate a form so people actually finish it
when_to_use: when the user is building any input form, signup, or settings page
body:
1. cut every field you can't justify - each one drops completion.
2. single column, labels above inputs, group related fields visually.
3. validate inline on blur, show the error next to the field that caused it.
4. match input type to data (email, tel, date) for the right keyboard.
5. make the submit button state the outcome and disable it only with a reason shown.

name: Onboarding Flow
description: design the first-run experience that gets users to value fast
when_to_use: when the user wants to design signup, setup, or a new-user welcome
body:
1. define the single "aha" moment and make the whole flow point at it.
2. defer every setup step that isn't required to reach that moment.
3. prefer showing-by-doing over upfront tours or modal walls.
4. let users skip, and persist progress so returns don't restart.
5. spec each step: goal, what's shown, the action, and the skip path.

name: Responsive Plan
description: decide how a layout adapts across screen sizes
when_to_use: when the user needs a design to work on mobile, tablet, and desktop
body:
1. design the narrow layout first - it forces priority decisions.
2. set breakpoints at content-break points, not at device widths.
3. for each breakpoint state what reflows, stacks, hides, or reveals.
4. keep touch targets >=44px and check thumb reach on the smallest screen.
5. note which elements stay fixed (nav, ctas) vs scroll with content.

name: Visual Hierarchy
description: order a screen so the eye lands on what matters first
when_to_use: when a layout feels flat, busy, or the user can't find the main action
body:
1. pick the one element that must win attention; everything else supports it.
2. build contrast with size, weight, and colour - not all three on everything.
3. use whitespace to group and separate; crowding flattens hierarchy.
4. limit to 2-3 type sizes and one accent so the loud thing stays loud.
5. verify by squinting - if the priority order isn't obvious, dial up contrast.

name: Interaction States
description: spec every state an interactive element can be in
when_to_use: when building a button, input, toggle, or any clickable component
body:
1. enumerate the states: default, hover, focus, active, disabled, loading, error.
2. for each, describe the visual change and what triggers it.
3. make focus distinct from hover so keyboard users aren't lost.
4. give disabled a reason (tooltip/helper) instead of a silent dead button.
5. cover the data states too: empty, partial, full, and overflow.

name: Heuristic Eval
description: score a product against nielsen's usability heuristics
when_to_use: when the user wants a structured expert review of an existing interface
body:
1. walk the core tasks while noting issues against each heuristic.
2. for every issue record: heuristic violated, location, and what the user feels.
3. rate severity 0-4 (cosmetic to catastrophe) so fixes can be triaged.
4. group duplicate issues so the list stays actionable, not noisy.
5. deliver a ranked table plus the top 3 to fix first.

name: Notification Design
description: decide what to notify, where, and how loud
when_to_use: when the user is adding alerts, toasts, badges, or push messages
body:
1. classify each event by urgency and whether it needs action.
2. pick the channel by urgency: inline < badge < toast < banner < push.
3. write the copy to lead with what changed and the action if any.
4. set dismissal and persistence - transient for info, sticky for decisions.
5. add a way to mute or batch so frequency never becomes noise.

name: Distinctive UI Design
description: make intentional visual design choices that don't read as templated defaults
when_to_use: when building or reviewing a ui that looks generic, bootstrap-y, or templated
body:
1. name the emotion the interface should leave - precision? warmth? speed?
2. pick one visual motif that carries that feeling and repeat it deliberately (edge style, spacing rhythm, type weight).
3. audit every default: rounded corners, gray palette, system font - override each one with intention, not aesthetics theatre.
4. reduce the palette to what's needed; strong hierarchy from fewer choices.
5. check the result looks like a decision was made, not a starter kit was accepted.

name: Color System
description: build a purposeful colour system from scratch or from a seed colour
when_to_use: when the user needs a colour scheme for a new product or wants to replace ad-hoc colours
body:
1. start from the brand mood or a single seed hue the user gives you.
2. derive a neutral ramp (9 stops, near-black to near-white) as the backbone.
3. pick one primary action colour and one signal colour; keep the rest neutral.
4. verify each pair used for text passes wcag aa before committing.
5. output as named css vars (--bg, --text, --accent, --signal, --surface).

name: Typography Scale
description: set a readable, harmonious type scale for a product
when_to_use: when the user is setting up type styles or fonts feel inconsistent across a product
body:
1. pick one or two fonts: one for headings, one for body (or the same at different weights).
2. set a base size (16px default) and a ratio (1.25 or 1.333) to generate sizes.
3. name stops by role: xs/sm/base/lg/xl/2xl - never by pixel.
4. set line-height per role: body 1.5-1.6, headings 1.1-1.2.
5. output as css vars and apply globally; ban one-off font-size rules in components.

name: Motion Design
description: add purposeful animation without making the ui feel slow
when_to_use: when the user wants to animate transitions, entrances, or state changes
body:
1. decide the role of each animation: guide attention, show state, give feedback.
2. keep durations short - 100ms for micro, 200-300ms for transitions, never over 500.
3. use ease-out for entrances, ease-in for exits, ease-in-out for state changes.
4. never animate layout or paint-heavy properties; stick to transform and opacity.
5. add prefers-reduced-motion: reduce guard to every keyframe or transition block.

name: Loading States
description: design what the ui shows while waiting for data or actions
when_to_use: when a screen, list, or action has async latency the user might see
body:
1. classify wait time: <100ms (nothing), 100-1000ms (spinner), >1s (skeleton).
2. skeleton screens match the final layout shape - no generic spinners for content.
3. for actions (submit, save) show inline feedback on the trigger element, not a modal.
4. lock the trigger once fired; re-enable on success or error with a message.
5. always have an error + retry path - loading is not a final state.

name: Component Audit
description: find duplicate and inconsistent ui components across a codebase or design
when_to_use: when a product has grown organically and components have drifted or multiplied
body:
1. list every unique button, input, card, and modal variant currently in use.
2. group near-identical ones and name the difference (size? colour? border?).
3. for each group, decide: merge into one with props, or keep distinct with a reason.
4. flag anything built from scratch that a library already provides.
5. output a table: component, variants to keep, variants to cut, effort to migrate.

name: Dark Mode
description: adapt a light-mode design to work well in dark mode
when_to_use: when the user wants to add or fix a dark theme for a ui
body:
1. don't invert: dark mode uses dark backgrounds, not flipped light ones.
2. replace pure black bg with a warm or cool near-black (~#141414) to reduce eye strain.
3. remap every colour token to its dark counterpart - borders go lighter, not darker.
4. check that shadows still read (they disappear on dark; use glow or overlay instead).
5. test text contrast at every level and adjust the neutral ramp for the dark context.

name: Error Handling UX
description: design clear, recoverable error experiences across a product
when_to_use: when the user is adding error handling to forms, api calls, or failed states
body:
1. classify errors: user fault (validation), transient (network), system (bug), permissions.
2. for each type: show it at the right scope (field / inline / page / toast).
3. write the message in plain language: what broke, why, and what to do.
4. never log-dump or show stack traces to users.
5. provide a recovery action on every error - retry, go back, or contact support.

name: Information Architecture
description: structure the content and navigation of a product so things are findable
when_to_use: when a product's nav is confusing, items are hard to find, or structure is being redesigned
body:
1. list all content types and features, then card-sort them into natural groups.
2. name each group from the user's vocabulary, not the company's internal language.
3. decide the depth: 2 levels max for nav; anything deeper needs search or shortcuts.
4. sketch the top-level nav and verify the 5 most common tasks land in <=2 clicks.
5. mark what's secondary - footer, overflow menu, advanced settings - so primary nav stays clean.

name: Spacing System
description: build a consistent spatial rhythm using a single base unit
when_to_use: when spacing across a ui is inconsistent or the user is setting up a layout system
body:
1. pick a base unit: 4px or 8px.
2. define the scale: 1x, 2x, 3x, 4x, 6x, 8x, 12x, 16x of that unit.
3. name steps by intent: gap-xs, gap-sm, gap-md, gap-lg, gap-xl.
4. map each step to a use: xs for icon padding, sm for inline gaps, md for field spacing, lg for section gaps.
5. enforce the scale as css vars and ban arbitrary values in component styles.
